Sometimes it feels like Ronaldo became more than just a footballer - almost a brand that major institutions had an interest in promoting. During the years when Messi was redefining what was possible on a football pitch, the narrative often seemed determined to keep the debate alive at all costs. That isn't to diminish Ronaldo's achievements, but it does raise questions when certain records receive enormous attention while others are quietly ignored.

For example, Ronaldo's 10 World Cup goals are frequently presented as part of an untouchable legacy, even though players like Harry Kane have reached the same figure in fewer tournaments. Meanwhile, Messi's World Cup numbers, overall playmaking, and trophy record often require a much higher standard of proof before receiving the same recognition.

The impression some fans get is that the football establishment spent years trying to maintain a rivalry that was becoming increasingly difficult to justify statistically. Whether intentional or not, the result was that Ronaldo's image was elevated into a global brand, while Messi's accomplishments were often treated as something that still needed defending. That's why the conversation sometimes feels less like a comparison of careers and more like a comparison between a footballer and a marketing machine.
